name: Charlie Rouse
abstract: Charlie Rouse (April 6, 1924 – November 30, 1988) was an American hard bop tenor saxophonist and flautist. His career is marked by his collaboration with Thelonious Monk, which lasted for more than ten years.

concerts.label: Bel Canto Foundation Jazz Concert to Benefit Musicians
Benefit Concert for the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: We had been part of the Ron Carter quartet... It was Ben who came up with the idea of Charlie Rouse... We got a gig and called Rouse. He made the gig, and it was a perfect fit... We existed for a long time, until Rouse passed away... We had a good run with that particular band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Weiss
about_quotes.evidence: And there’s no better example than Monk. When you listen to the way Charlie Rouse for example, plays Monk’s melodies with him...
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Weiss interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 22, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: Charlie Rouse -- we formed a band, a six-piece band. We worked at this famous club... called the Crystal Caverns. ... when I had the little band with Charlie Rouse and Phil Phillips and Osie Johnson and Tommy Potter and Harry Curtis... This was 1942.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: They had Phil Woods, Coleman Hawkins, Rouse, I forget his first -- Charlie Rouse, and Bennie Carter, and I forget the others. Five saxophones. So it was all like supersax, you know. Now, this was in the sixties, you know.

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Carter
about_quotes.evidence: so we got together, oh, Phil Woods on the other alto and Charlie Rouse on the other tenor, Coleman Hawkins, myself ... we made that 'Further Definitions.'

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Wormworth
about_quotes.evidence: Charlie Rouse came up to me and asked me if I was interested in working with his group... And I worked with them for, I don’t know, a couple of years
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Wormworth interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002
